idrāk.key_passage: The human being is neither spirit nor body, but rather the perception (idrāk) generated between them when they come together.
idrāk.key_ar: الإنسان ليس هو الروح ولا هو الجسم، بل هو الإدراك الحاصل بينهما عند اجتماعهما
idrāk.key_point: Al-Tijānī's final, revised anthropology: the human being is idrāk — the awareness that arises when rūḥ and jism meet. Neither alone constitutes the human being.
idrāk.source: Mishri, Kitāb al-Jāmiʿ, reporting from Shaykh Aḥmad al-Tijānī

rūḥ (Tijānī).key_passage: From the moment God created it [the rūḥ], it has been knowing, aware, remembering, and has never ceased from the remembrance of God for even the blink of an eye.
rūḥ (Tijānī).key_point: Al-Tijānī: the rūḥ is eternally aware and never heedless — making it unsuitable as the locus of moral accountability. Accountability belongs to the idrāk (the human being properly).
rūḥ (Tijānī).source: Mishri, Kitāb al-Jāmiʿ, reporting from Shaykh Aḥmad al-Tijānī

awliyāʾ (perception after death).key_passage: As for God's chosen elect among His servants, perception does not leave their bodies when the spirit departs, just as it remains with their spirits. For this reason, it is reported that they continue worshipping God after death just as they did before it.
awliyāʾ (perception after death).key_point: The awliyāʾ are distinguished by the permanence of idrāk — perception remains in both rūḥ and jism after death, giving continuity of worship.
awliyāʾ (perception after death).source: Mishri, Kitāb al-Jāmiʿ, reporting from Shaykh Aḥmad al-Tijānī
